1: Minimally recognized: Some individuals are familiar with them
2: Clear awareness: Sustainability is recognized as relevant
4: Basic management: Some informal reuse or disposal practices
6: Partial inventory: Some datasets are documented
7: Considered: Scalability is part of system design
9: Good: Software remains usable for expected lifespan (e.g., 3–5 years) with minimal hardware changes
10: Mostly clean: Can be removed with minor traces remaining
11: Ad hoc: Occasionally considered, but not systematically or documented
12: Flexible: Modular design and supports integration with other systems
13: Limited: Some monitoring exists, but not continuous or not easily accessible
14: Reactive: Occasional optimization based on identified issues
15: Informal: Occasional or ad hoc sharing within teams
16: Limited: Occasional sharing (e.g., informal exchanges or isolated initiatives)
17: Limited documentation: Some information exists, but not necessarly structured and must be aware to find it.
18: Static documentation: Documentation exists but is not often updated.
19: Not encouraged: Sustainability is not part of daily work.
20: Occasionally recognized: When a major improvement is made.
21: Integrated: Some Values are embedded in multiple areas of the organization.
22: Partially defined: Some key roles include sustainability responsibilities.
23: No, we do not want that.
24: No training: No training is provided.
25: Not included: Not part of development programs.
26: Limited introduction: Yes it is mentioned introduction.
27: We occasionally come across sustainability improvements, but there is no structured effort to stay updated.
29: Sustainability might be talked about but not actively evaluated.
30: Policies exist but are informal or undocumented.
31: Informal goals: We have informal or unclear sustainability goals.
33: Opportunistic action: We reduce impact only when convenient or as a side effect of other changes.
